w:r.w:t: ScribeCount Website Traffic
Scope of Work (SOW)
ScribeCount Website Traffic & Behavior Intelligence Service
First-Party Web Analytics, Conversions, Heatmaps, and Unified Reporting
Document Version:
vNext
(Architecture & Integration Focus)
Client:
ScribeCount
Service Name (Working):
ScribeCount Web Traffic
Target Users:
Independent authors and small author teams
Primary Goal:
Privacy-first website intelligence that merges cleanly with ScribeCount sales, email, and attribution data to produce actionable author insights
1) Executive Summary

ScribeCount will deliver a first-party Website Traffic and Behavior Intelligence service designed specifically for independent authors. The service will collect, normalize, and analyze traffic and engagement data from author-owned websites and storefronts, beginning with WordPress, Shopify, and Wix.
This feature is not intended to replicate generic third-party analytics platforms. Instead, it is engineered to act as
one of
ScribeCount’s
core signal sources

, capturing reader intent and behavior on author websites and making that data usable across the ScribeCount ecosystem—particularly in reporting, attribution, email performance, and future recommendation systems.
A defining requirement of this service is
data sovereignty
. All tracking is first-party, privacy-first, and processed exclusively within ScribeCount infrastructure. The output of the service is not raw analytics dashboards alone, but
structured behavioral data
that integrates with
ScribeCount’s
existing reporting model so authors can understand how marketing actions translate into outcomes.
In practical terms, the Website Traffic feature enables authors—and ScribeCount—to answer questions that are currently fragmented across multiple tools:
Which marketing channels send
engaged
readers, not just clicks?
Which pages actually lead to signups, buy clicks, or purchases?
Where do readers hesitate or drop off before converting?
How do email campaigns, ads, and website behavior interact?
What changed yesterday that deserves attention today?
2) Objective (Expanded Narrative)
2.1 What the Feature Is
The Website Traffic feature is a
first-party analytics and behavior intelligence system
tailored to how authors use websites as part of their publishing business.
It includes:
Website traffic measurement (sessions, visitors, pageviews, engagement)
Acquisition analysis (source, medium, campaign, referrer)
Content performance (landing pages, top pages, exit behavior where available)
Conversion tracking (newsletter signups, buy clicks, purchases when supported)
Funnel analysis (multi-step reader journeys)
Heatmaps (click and scroll behavior)
Daily summary snapshots for routine decision-making
An internal data API that allows other ScribeCount systems to consume web behavior signals securely
This feature treats the author’s website as a
decision surface
, not merely a content container. The goal is to make website data explain
why
other metrics inside ScribeCount move the way they do.
2.2 How It Should Be Constructed
The system must be designed with
integration longevity
as a primary constraint. As with ScribeCount Email, Website Traffic is a standalone operational service, but
ScribeCount remains the unified reporting and intelligence hub
Construction principles include:
Clear separation between collection, normalization, analytics, and reporting
Event-first design, where every meaningful user interaction produces a structured event
Strong schema discipline so data can join reliably with email, sales, and attribution datasets
Versioned internal contracts so downstream features can evolve safely
Observability and auditability suitable for support, compliance, and debugging
The Website Traffic service should not “own” business conclusions. It owns
behavioral facts
. Interpretation and recommendations belong to
ScribeCount’s
reporting and intelligence layers.
2.3 How Authors Will Use It
Authors want clarity, not configuration overhead.
From the author’s perspective, the experience should feel like:
Connect site → confirm tracking works
See meaningful data appear quickly
Understand what’s working and what isn’t
Make better decisions without juggling tools
Typical author workflows supported by this feature include:
Evaluating whether ads, social posts, or emails drive meaningful site engagement
Identifying which landing pages and book pages actually convert
Understanding whether mobile readers behave differently than desktop readers
Improving page layout and CTA placement using heatmaps
Monitoring daily performance changes without logging into multiple systems
The feature must support
multi-site authors
(main site, series sites, direct store domains) while presenting data in a way that remains comprehensible and actionable.
3) How Website Traffic Data Integrates
Into
ScribeCount
The most important architectural requirement is that Website Traffic is not an isolated analytics silo. It is a
shared behavioral data source
designed to integrate cleanly with existing and future ScribeCount features.
3.1 Integration with Email Marketing (Immediate)
Website Traffic and ScribeCount Email are designed as complementary systems.
Website data enables email reporting to answer higher-value questions:
Did an email click lead to meaningful on-site engagement?
Which email campaigns drive signups versus immediate buy clicks?
Do certain segments behave differently once they reach the site?
Conversely, email metadata (campaigns, flows, link groups) provides context that makes website traffic intelligible. This bidirectional relationship allows ScribeCount to report on
email → site → outcome
as a single narrative, rather than disconnected metrics.
3.2 Integration with Sales and Attribution
Website Traffic produces critical intermediary signals between marketing actions and sales outcomes:
Landing page effectiveness before a purchase
Buy-click behavior even when purchases occur off-site
Funnel drop-offs that explain sales volatility
When combined with
ScribeCount’s
existing sales and retailer click-out data, this enables more accurate and more honest attribution—clearly distinguishing confirmed outcomes from directional signals.
3.3 Integration with Daily Email and Alerts
The Website Traffic feature will generate a
daily snapshot
per site that feeds directly into the existing ScribeCount daily email.
This snapshot is not a full report; it is a decision aid. It highlights:
Core KPIs and their movement
Top traffic sources and pages
Conversion activity
Anomalies that may warrant action
By making website behavior part of the daily ScribeCount rhythm, the feature increases product engagement while reinforcing
ScribeCount’s
role as the author’s operational dashboard.
3.4 Integration with Future Intelligence and Recommendations
Website Traffic lays the foundation for future ScribeCount capabilities, including:
Automated optimization suggestions (page layout, CTA placement, funnel sequencing)
Smarter campaign recommendations based on historical behavior patterns
Content strategy insights (what topics or pages consistently convert)
Cohort analysis tied to reader acquisition sources
Because the system exports normalized data through an internal API, these features can be added incrementally without re-architecting the traffic system itself.
4) Target Architecture (Conceptual, Integration-First)
4.1 Core Components
A) First-Party Collection Layer
Responsible for capturing on-site behavior using a ScribeCount-controlled tracking mechanism. Enforces privacy, consent, and redaction rules at the edge.
B) Ingestion & Normalization Layer
Validates incoming events, applies enrichment (device, coarse geo), and produces normalized behavioral events aligned to
ScribeCount’s
shared event vocabulary.
C) Analytics & Aggregation Layer
Optimized for behavioral queries, rollups, and trend analysis. Produces query-ready datasets for dashboards, snapshots, and downstream services.
D) Heatmap Aggregation Layer
Aggregates interaction data into privacy-safe heatmaps without session replay or keystroke capture.
E) Snapshot & Reporting Layer
Produces immutable daily summaries and supports fast dashboard rendering.
F) Internal API & Export Layer (ScribeCount-Only)
Provides secure, scoped access to web metrics and events for other ScribeCount systems. Enforces tenant isolation, versioning, and rate limits.
4.2 Construction Guidance (No Code)
Design for
site-scoped isolation
with tenant-level governance
Normalize early so downstream services never need provider-specific logic
Prefer immutable reporting artifacts (snapshots) for reproducibility
Treat event schemas as contracts, not implementation details
Build connector modules as adapters, not special cases
5) Benefits
5.1 Benefits to Authors
One place to understand website performance and reader behavior
Clear visibility into what marketing efforts actually convert
Practical optimization tools (funnels, heatmaps) without invasive tracking
Reduced dependence on third-party analytics tools
Daily insight without analysis paralysis
5.2 Benefits to ScribeCount
A high-value behavioral signal that strengthens all reporting
Increased daily engagement and platform stickiness
A foundation for premium analytics and recommendation features
Strong privacy-first positioning in a post-third-party-cookie landscape
Cleaner, more defensible attribution narratives
6) Guiding Principles and Constraints
First-party, privacy-first collection only
No fingerprinting or sensitive data capture
No outbound data exports beyond ScribeCount
Architecture-first documentation; no sample code
Designed for integration with Email, Sales, Attribution, and future features
7) Acceptance Criteria (High-Level)
A release is acceptable when:
Authors can connect a site and verify tracking reliably
Behavioral data appears within expected freshness windows
Website metrics integrate cleanly into ScribeCount dashboards and daily email
Conversions and funnels behave consistently across providers
Heatmaps respect privacy constraints and render accurately
Internal APIs provide reliable, scoped access to downstream services
8) Future-Proofing
The Website Traffic service must support future ScribeCount enhancements without major refactors, including:
Additional site platforms
Deeper attribution modeling
AI-assisted optimization insights
Advanced cohort and lifecycle analysis
Tighter integration with direct sales tooling
